title: Slopes and Rates of Change
concept: A derivative measures the rate of change of a function, showing how much a tiny adjustment to an input alters the output.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In a machine learning dataset, what does each feature represent in a high-dimensional vector space?

options.text: A completely new, perpendicular direction or axis in space
A duplicate copy of the target column

exercises.explain: Exactly! Each feature acts as a new axis or direction in a high-dimensional space. Now, how do we measure changes in this space?

cards.title: Stepping on the gas
cards.body: When you press the gas pedal in a car, you aren't just changing your position — you're changing your speed. You are experiencing a rate of change.

cards.title: What is a derivative?
cards.body: In calculus, a derivative is a measure of how much an output changes when you make a tiny change to the input. It is the slope of the curve at a single point.

cards.title: The slope under your shoe
cards.body: Think of a derivative like the slope of a hill under your shoe. It's not the height of the mountain, but how steep the ground is right where you are standing.

cards.title: Nudging square footage
cards.body: If a house price model has a derivative of 200 with respect to size, it means adding one tiny unit of space increases the predicted price by $200.

cards.title: Not just for straight lines
cards.body: You might think a slope only makes sense for straight lines. But calculus lets us find the slope of curvy lines, too, by zooming in so close that the curve looks perfectly straight.

left.text:
Derivative is near zero
Input changes have no effect

right.text:
Derivative is high
Tiny input changes cause big shifts

options.text: How much the output changes when you make a tiny change to the input
The total sum of all outputs in the dataset
The average value of the inputs

exercises.explain: A derivative tells you the rate of change — how sensitive the output is to a tiny tweak in the input.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A curve can have different slopes (derivatives) at different points.
exercises.explain: True! Unlike a straight line, a curve's steepness changes depending on where you are on it.

exercises.sentence: If a tiny increase in a model's weight causes a huge drop in prediction error, the derivative of the error with respect to that weight is ___.
exercises.explain: A large output change from a tiny input nudge means the derivative (rate of change) is high.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario describes a derivative of zero?

options.text: You turn a knob on a radio, but the volume doesn't change at all
You press the gas pedal, and the car speeds up rapidly
You add sugar to a recipe, and it suddenly tastes much sweeter

exercises.explain: When changes to the input have zero effect on the output, the rate of change (derivative) is zero.

exercises.prompt: You are adjusting the threshold of a spam filter. Nudging the threshold by a tiny 0.01 increases spam detection by a massive 5%. What does this tell you about the derivative of detection rate with respect to the threshold?

options.text: The derivative is high, meaning the filter is highly sensitive to changes in the threshold
The derivative is zero, meaning the threshold has no effect
The derivative is negative, meaning the filter is broken

exercises.explain: Outstanding! A large response to a tiny nudge means a high derivative (high sensitivity). You've mastered how derivatives measure rates of change! Next up: finding peaks and valleys!
